citation.title: Development of a robust ion-selective membrane from sulfonated cellulose nanofibers for zinc–iodine redox flow batteries

content: A B S T R A C T Electrochemical cells play a critical role across various sectors, enabling technologies in renewable energy generation and storage, environmental monitoring, and management. Among different options, redox flow batteries (RFBs) are gaining importance for large-scale energy storage applications due mainly to their decoupled power and energy outputs associated with easily scalable design. As part of acidic and neutral RFBs, proton exchange membranes (PEMs) are essential for transport of cations, preventing the mixing of anolyte and cath­olyte while still allowing high ion conductivity. However, many commercial PEMs are made of poly and per­fluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) and besides being expensive and petroleum feedstock-derived, they raise significant environmental concerns. In this work, we developed high-efficiency sulfonated nanocellulose mem­branes (S–CNF) incorporating layer-by-layer assembly silanization modification. The S–CNF membranes exhibited excellent thermal-oxidative stability (up to 250 ◦C), dimensional stability, and mechanical strength (Young’s modulus reaching 1.4 GPa and storage modulus exceeding 1.1 GPa). Additionally, they demonstrated high moisture-uptake (absorbing up to 90 % water after 48 h). A zinc-iodine redox flow battery (ZIRFB) assembled with S–CNF membranes achieved an average coulombic, voltage and energy efficiencies of 98 %, 66 %, and 65 %, respectively, at a current density of 20 mA cm-2.

Overall, the S–CNF membranes developed in this study offer a bio-based, simple, cost-effective, and durable alternative to conventional PEMs, with promising scalability for energy storage applications in ZIRFBs.

citation.title: Development of a robust ion-selective membrane from sulfonated cellulose nanofibers for zinc–iodine redox flow batteries

content: 1. Introduction A proton exchange membrane (PEM), also referred to in a more general sense as a cation exchange membrane (CEM), is a semiperme­able membrane designed to selectively transport cations along its polymeric backbone. PEMs are used in different electrochemical sys­tems, such as in fuel cells (FCs), water electrolysis systems, or RFBs (redox flow batteries). In an RFB, the PEM can also prevent the mixing of anolyte and catholyte [1–4]. Among different types of RFBs, ZIRFBs (Zinc-iodine redox flow bat­teries) are developing rapidly and have become one of the promising options for large-scale energy storage, due to several inherent advan­tages [5]. Firstly, zinc is an abundant element and forms non-toxic, non-flammable aqueous electrolytes, with adequate ionic conductivity [6]. Additionally, the electrolyte made with I-/I3-has higher solubility (compared to other halide salts such as ZnCl2 and ZnBr2), and the standard cell potential of the Zn/I3-cell is 1.3 V at 298 K that minimizes the water electrolysis reactions. Furthermore, the ZIRFB has a higher theoretical energy density compared to Zn-Bromine and all-vanadium chemistries (~322 Wh L-1 at the solubility limit of ZnI2 in water (4500 g/L, 7.0 M) by assuming that all iodide species are fully converted into triiodide in the catholyte upon charging and that the anolyte does not participate in the redox reactions) [7,8].

The latter, due to the low selectivity of the membrane, can promote self-discharge and capacity degradation [5,11]. Additionally, a satisfactory membrane should be strong enough to resist mechanical failure due to the zinc dendrite formation on its surface. Most RFB systems utilize polymers containing perand poly­fluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), like Nafion, which has a hydrophobic tetrafluoroethylene backbone interlaced with hydrophilic sulfonic acid groups mainly responsible for the ion transport [12]. However, PFAS membranes have some limitations in terms of their chemical and ther­mal durability [13], dimensional instability, and crossover of negative ions and neutral molecules [14]. Depending on the RFB system, PFAS membranes have moderate to low ion selectivity, are highly costly, and are dangerous for the environment. For instance, Nafion membranes are expensive (>$500 m-2 for commercial Nafion series) [15], and its degradation generates PFAS, which are highly toxic environmental pollutants [16].

content: are expensive (>$500 m-2 for commercial Nafion series) [15], and its degradation generates PFAS, which are highly toxic environmental pollutants [16]. These issues reaffirm the importance of designing alternative membranes that minimize these problems and guarantee the development of a stable and sustainable large-scale energy storage system. A variety of biopolymeric materials have been explored as alterna­tives to synthetic PEMs, including: cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs) [17,18]; Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) [19–21]; bacterial cellulose [22,23]; so­dium alginate [24,25]; lignin and chitosan [26–28]. Among these, cel­lulose and modified cellulose have gained significant attention due to their high wettability, good mechanical properties, thermal stability, and tunable porosity and thickness—all of which are critical for ion-exchange membranes (IEMs). Lander et al. (2022) [29] synthesized cross-linked sulfonated cellu­lose nanofibers (S–CNF) membranes for aqueous organic redox flow batteries (AORFBs). These membranes exhibited low porosity and water uptake and achieved a coulombic efficiency (CE) of ~84 % and a discharge capacity of 8 mAh at 4 mA cm-2. Similarly, Mukhopadhyay et al. (2019) [30] developed a stable, highly ion-selective membrane by incorporating proton-conductive cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) into a hydrophobic poly(vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene) (PVDF-HFP) matrix.

The resulting vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) achieved a CE of 98.2 %, an energy efficiency (EE) of 88.2 %, and a voltage efficiency (VE) of 89.8 % at a current density of 100 mA cm-2. A few studies reported in the literature have explored the use of bio-based and non-bio-based membrane materials in ZIRFB systems to enhance battery performance. For example, Yang et al. (2020) [31] synthesized a multifunctional metal–organic framework (MOF) mem­brane (Zn-BTC), which provided simultaneous improvements to both the iodine cathode and the Zn anode in high-performance aqueous Zn–I2 batteries. The results showed that the battery assembled with the Zn-BTC membrane achieved a lifespan of 6000 cycles, a capacity retention of 84.6 %, and a CE of 99.65 %. Tangthuam et al. (2020) [32] prepared polyelectrolyte membranes from carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), demonstrating that higher PVA content enhanced ionic conductivity but also increased iodine and pol­yiodide crossover. Another example is the work by Williams et al. (2023) [33], who fabricated sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone) (SPEEK) membranes. Their results showed a CE and EE of 92.9 % and 75.9 %, respectively, at 17 mA/cm2 when applied in a ZIRFB, which represents a 370 % increase in energy density compared to batteries using Nafion 212 membranes.

content: pectively, at 17 mA/cm2 when applied in a ZIRFB, which represents a 370 % increase in energy density compared to batteries using Nafion 212 membranes. The study also reported a peak power density of 122 mW/cm2 at a current density of 166 mA/cm2 (compared to 98 mW/cm2 at 133 mA/cm2 for Nafion 212). In this study, we synthesized membranes based on sulfonated cel­lulose nanofibers (S–CNF membranes) and modified their surface by layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly and silanization. We hypothesize that these surface functionalization strategies would: (1) Enhance membrane ion selectivity, reducing iodine species diffusion; (2) improve battery efficiency, particularly in terms of CE, EE, and VE and, (3) increase discharge capacity, making the system more viable for large-scale en­ergy storage. This is the first study to explore the combined effects of LbL and silanization in bio-based PEMs for ZIRFB applications, offering a sustainable alternative to PFAS-based materials. Additionally, we comprehensively evaluated the morphology, mechanical and thermal stability, chemical structure, water uptake, ion exchange capacity (IEC), and proton conductivity of the developed membranes. 2. Precursor fiber processing Cellulose fibers were first fibrillated with a laboratory PFI mill. After dispersion at a solid content of 10 wt%, the pulp was refined for 10,000 revolutions, until reaching a value of 100 CSF according to ISO 5267-2 standard (68 ◦SR). The fibrillated fibers were then diluted to 1 wt% for further modification. The sulfonation of the fibers was performed following the method­ology described previously [34,35] and fibrillated into CNF using a M -110P microfluidizer (Microfluidics, USA) equipped with an 87 μm Z-type chamber for 5 passes with a gradually increasing pressure from 1700 bar to 2000 bar.

Prior to the fibrillation, the sulfonated fibers were diluted to 0.63 wt% and the pH was adjusted to 8.0 with 3 M NaOH. The characterization of the S–CNF fibers can be found in our previous report [34]. 2.3. Membrane fabrication The sulfonated CNF (S–CNF) membranes were prepared by filtration and hot pressing using a previously outlined procedure [36]. The S–CNF suspension was diluted to 0.5 wt% and degassed under vacuum for 2 h to remove air. Homogenization ensured a complete mixing of the S–CNF fibers, using an Ultra-Turrax T-25 disperser (IKA, Germany) at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The suspension was then filtered with a 142 mm Büchner funnel equipped with a 0.22 μm PVDF membrane (Merck, Germany), until the S–CNF mat exhibited a semi-dry aspect (solid content of approx. 15 wt%). The following step used decompression filtration (Vacuubrand, Model: MD 4C NT + AK + EK at 1.5 mbar) to prepare all S–CNF modified membranes. The S–CNF mat was placed between the PVDF membrane on one side and a 1-μm nylon sieve (Sefar, Switzerland) on the other side, while blotting papers were added above it. This stack was then dried by hot pressing at 70 ◦C and 0.1 bar for 1h, until complete removal of water from the S–CNF mat was achieved. The resulting dry membranes were stored at 23 ◦C and 50 %RH for 48 h before characterization. 2.4.

All samples were heated to 800 ◦C at 10 ◦C min-1, with a nitrogen flow rate of 20 cm3 min-1 over two repetitions (Fig. S2). Dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) was performed with a TA Q850. The membranes were cut into 0.5 cm * 2 cm rectangles. The tests were carried out with a frequency of 1Hz, 1 N applied pre-force, and an oscillation amplitude of 10 μm. The storage modulus (E’) and tan delta were obtained at a heating rate of 3 ◦C min-1 in air with a temperature range from 20 to 280 ◦C, and in an oscillatory tensile mode with hu­midity control. Throughout the experiment, relative humidity was held constant at 90 %. The temperature was raised from 30 to ≈80 ◦C. This analysis was performed in duplicate (Fig. S2).

The cellulose tricarbanilates (CTCs) were obtained from sulfonated cellulose using the methodology pro­posed by Kaˇcík et al., 2009 [42]. 20 mg of dry S–CNF was mixed with 6 mL of dry pyridine for 24 h. Then, 1 mL of phenylisocyanate was added under nitrogen atmosphere to avoid the phenylisocyanate reacting with oxygen. This mixture was stirred for 48 h at 80 ◦C. The resulting clear yellow viscous solution was cooled; excess of phenylisocyanate was decomposed by adding 2 mL of methanol and then the final solution was slowly dropwise into rapidly stirred methanol. The precipitate was washed with water containing acetic acid to remove pyridine, and then with water and methanol and finally dried at 50 ◦C in a vacuum oven.

The sulfonation of CNF aims to improve the electrochemical prop­erties of the resulting bio-based PEMs by introducing SO3-groups that form hydrophilic domains upon hydration. Through these groups, the membrane’s nanostructure enables ionic conduction, allowing hydrogen ions to move freely in regions where sufficient water is present. It is also observed that these materials, especially PDADMAC, form a thicker and more non-homogeneous coating on the substrate at concentrations of 10 g/L and above. According to Table 2, almost all bio-based membranes have similar or higher mechanical and thermal stability than Nafion 212, showing potential application due to their mechanical and thermal stability at the studied temperature range. Both TGA and DMA analyses are essential for verifying whether the thermal and mechanical stability of the bio-based PEM align with Nafion’s operational conditions. According to the literature, Nafion’s maximum temperature efficiency typically ranges from 80 to 90 ◦C under fully hydrated conditions. Below 80 ◦C, proton conductivity de­creases due to reduced ion mobility, and water management becomes more challenging, potentially leading to flooding. Conversely, above 90 ◦C, the membrane begins to lose mechanical and chemical stability, and proton conductivity drops significantly due to dehydration (as Nafion relies on water molecules for proton conduction). As seen in Fig. 6a, both water uptake and hydration number increase as the number of layers and the concentration of PDADMAC and MMT rise from 0.1 g/L to 10 g/L (with the number of layers held constant), considering the hydrophilic nature of both materials. A slight increase in these properties is observed when the concentration rises from 1 g/L to 10 g/L. However, a significant drop occurs when the sample becomes hydrophobic due to the silane external layer (see Fig. S5 for contact angle measurements). Except for the S–CNF–Hydrophobic membrane, there is no significant difference in proton conductivity and IEC for the other samples. As previously mentioned, the hydration number refers to the number of water molecules associated with each ionic group in the membrane. As shown in Figs. 1 and 2, even after sulfonation, the S–CNF still contains OH– groups in its molecular chain, and both groups can contribute to increasing the hydration number, water uptake, and con­ductivity. However, the proton conductivity of a PEM is also influenced by the dissociation constants of its ionic groups. For instance, the dissociation constant associated with the deprotonation of SO3-groups is higher than that of OH–, which means the pKa of SO3-groups is lower than that of OH-.

As a result, the OH-groups do not have the same impact on the membrane’s conductivity as it has on the water uptake and hydration number. In addition, the lower conductivity of the biobased membrane can be attributed to the weaker acidic nature of the sulfonic acid groups in S–CNF compared with the super-acidic behavior of the fluorinated sulfonic acid side chains in Nafion [40]. According to Bayer et al. (2021) [40], this is due to the higher activation energy required for proton conduction in S–CNF (0.26 eV) compared to that in Nafion (~0.16 eV). The more hydrophobic nature of this sample reduced water inter­action, consequently decreasing its ability to transport cations through the membrane. The chemical stability of the S–CNF membranes was investigated using Fenton’s reagent for 1 h at 80 ◦C. All samples have oxidative stability higher than 80 % and the surface functionalization also contributes to increasing this property (Table 2). The higher con­ductivity of Nafion 212 (Table 2), compared to S–CNF, is directly related to the large amount and the high acidity of the sulfonic groups as well as the presence of more oriented confined channels between its hydro­phobic and hydrophilic domains. The difference between CNF and Nafion is likely due to the use of functional groups that are significantly less acidic [18].

Application of the S–CNF membrane in a zinc iodine redox flow battery All membranes, as described in Table 1, were used for the diffusion test to evaluate the I3-crossover over time (Fig. 7a). This species has a red color when dissolved in water, providing a visual indicator to determine whether iodine species cross the membrane during the experiment. Determining the level of I3-crossover is crucial because this anion reacts with the solid zinc electrode, causing a self-discharge effect that reduces cell capacity and efficiencies (CE, EE, and VE). Therefore, the membrane with the lowest crossover in this test is likely the best candidate for battery cycling. The results of this test are shown in Fig. 7b. Fig. 7b shows that the I3-crossover decreases as the number of bi­layers increases and the concentrations of PDADMAC and MMT changes from 0.1 g/L to 1 g/L. The PDADMAC and MMT molecules fill the pores on the surface of the S–CNF membranes and the spaces between the cellulose fibers in the bulk of the membrane.

As more layers of both components are added, the I3-concentration in container 1 lowered during the charging step (Fig. 7b). However, when the concentrations of PDADMAC and MMT increase from 1 g/L to 10 g/L, I3-crossover rises significantly. At higher concentrations, these species form a weak film that creates channels and larger pores, which leads to cellulose separa­tion and increases I3-crossover. The S–CNF–Hydrophobic membrane shows the lowest crossover due to the silanization step following the LbL treatment (Fig. 7b). The hydrophobic silane acts as a water-repellent coating on the iodine side, preventing water molecules from bringing I3-through diffusion and solvation. Based on this initial study, the membrane with the potential for best performance in a ZIRFB is the hydrophobic PEM with 25 layers (since there is no significant difference between the 25-layer and 40-layer membranes) at a 1 g/L concentration of PDADMAC and MMT, labeled as S–CNF–25L–Hydrophobic. To further evaluate its effectiveness as a PEM for energy storage applications, the membrane was integrated into a ZIRFB configuration and tested. The charge and discharge curves of the ZIRFB setup using the S–CNF – 25L –Hydrophobic are shown in Fig. 8, where its performance can also be compared with that of the S–CNF–10L–Hydrophobic and Nafion 212 membranes.

content: in a vanadium redox flow battery [53], and 0.018 Ah L-1 for sulfonated cellulose applied in an all-quinone aqueous organic redox flow batteries [37]. Note that the capacity of any battery depends on several factors, such as the quantity of active material, the number and physical dimensions of the plates, and the specific gravity of the electrolyte, as well as the battery’s operational conditions. The latter includes the battery’s load, discharge rate, depth of discharge, cut-off voltage, temperature, and cycle history [54]. Therefore, the capacity values reported here or elsewhere cannot be solely attributed to the membrane as the only determining factor in battery performance. The performance of a PEM is critically influenced by both its bulk properties (e.g., ion conductivity, thickness, water uptake) and interfa­cial properties (e.g., surface roughness, hydrophilicity), which directly impact CE, EE, and VE. For instance, high proton conductivity in the bulk of the membrane ensures efficient ionic transfer, reducing resistive ohmic losses and improving CE, EE, and VE. On the other hand, the membrane’s thickness and porosity influence the crossover of reactants (e.g., hydrogen, oxygen, or other species). For example, a thicker membrane may reduce crossover but increase resistance (which reduces CE, EE, and VE), whereas a thinner membrane may increase crossover, leading to side reactions and lower CE. Another example involves the surface area and morphology of a PEM.

content: a thinner membrane may increase crossover, leading to side reactions and lower CE. Another example involves the surface area and morphology of a PEM. In this case, a rough surface may increase the probability of reactant crossover (e.g., I3-crossover in a ZIRFB) or trapping of reactants, leading to side reactions and energy losses, which decrease CE and EE, respectively [55,56]. This research does not include a degradation study to determine how many cycles the membranes could withstand before needing replace­ment. Over the course of 100 cycles, the zinc dendrite formation was observed in the tubes and posolyte solution, which could lead to decay in battery efficiency. Zinc dendrites form due to the unequal distribution of active species and charges, resulting in the creation of dead zinc. This reduces the utilization efficiency of active species and negatively im­pacts on the cycle stability of the battery [51,57,58]. In summary, the cycle life was limited by the Zn side. Besides the zinc dendrite formation, the ZIRFB also suffers from accumulation of undissolved I2 that combined with dead Zn can punc­ture the membrane, and clog the flow cell inlets and outlets, causing the system to leak or rupture [6]. Thus, the lower concentrations (1 M) and shorter charge/discharge times (30 min each) were used to properly evaluate the membrane characteristics in this system, without the need to account for any experimental artefacts caused by these issues.

The incorporation of –SO3- groups was not enough to obtain membranes with high performance electrochemical properties (based on the results of IEC and proton conductivity), but the surface func­tionalization steps undertaken here, LbL and silanization, increased the ion selectivity of the bio-based PEM, and consequently, reduced the I3- migration (Figs. 2 and 3). All synthesized membranes exhibited excel­lent mechanical, thermal, and chemical stability as well as high hydro­philic and barrier properties when compared to a commercial membrane (Table 2). Based on the diffusion and battery results, the S–CNF–25L–Hydrophobic sample demonstrated a high energy storage capacity in terms of CE, EE, VE, and discharge capacity compared to the S–CNF–10L–Hydrophobic PEM. Although the membrane did not show the same or higher performance as Nafion, this bio-based material pri­marily composed of cellulose, exhibited values very close to Nafion, with differences of less than 2 % for some of the evaluated properties (Table S1) and with stable performance over the 100-cycle battery test. The modified CNF membranes developed in the present study display comparable values to some bio-based ion-exchange membranes re­ported in the literature, even at different test conditions, as outlined in Table 3.

Examples include membranes composed of bacterial cellulose and PFSA, which exhibit a CE of 89.03 %, a VE of 88.62 %, and an EE of 78.92 % at 80 mA/cm2, after 50 cycles in a vanadium redox flow battery with the upper and lower limits of the charging and discharging voltages fixed at 1.7 and 1.0 V, respectively [60]. Additionally, a sulfonated CNF membrane applied in an aqueous organic redox flow battery demon­strated a CE around 95 % at 4 mA cm-2 (Lander et al., 2022) [29]. Although some examples show better performance than the system proposed in this work, many of them rely on synthetic polymer back­bones (e.g. perfluorosulfonic acid, poly(ether ether ketone). Here, the bio-based PEM was essentially synthesized using cellulose with very low concentration of artificial materials (PDADMAC, MMT, and silane). The hypothesis proposed in this research is that the combination of two simple and straightforward modification steps, LbL and hydro­phobization, can reduce the iodine species crossover and improve the performance of a S–CNF membrane in terms of its capacity to storage energy as applied in a ZIRFB system. Essentially, the –SO3- groups alter the conductivity of the membrane, by providing more charge sites and channels for cation transport in the bulk of the membrane.

However, by creating an external hydrophobic layer, the I3-concentration on the opposite side of a ZIRFB decreases, resulting in a higher CE, VE, and discharge capacity. The findings presented in this research introduce a bio-based mate­rial with significant thermal, mechanical, and chemical stability (Table 2, Table S1, and Fig. 8), along with a superhydrophilic capacity that enables a PEM to absorb water while its conductivity progressively increases. Additionally, the S–CNF – 25L – Hydrophobic PEM showed comparable battery results to Nafion 212 membrane, reinforcing cellu­lose efficiency when also used in energy systems. Although the modified CNF membranes display comparable values to other bio-based systems listed in Table 3, we recognize that they still need to achieve higher energy efficiency and longer cycling lifespans, especially when the battery is operated at higher current densities. To address this, our research group is conducting ongoing studies to further enhance the membrane’s performance and stability in order to bring a bio-based PEM to a commercial level. As a potential research area, we emphasize the importance of incorporating cellulosic materials into PEM composition to address existing gaps in the current commercial membranes such as high costs and high environmental impact. 4.

Conclusion This research proposes a simple yet effective approach for devel­oping a robust ion exchange membrane utilizing sulfonated cellulose nanofibrils (S–CNF) for zinc-iodine redox flow battery (ZIRFB) appli­cations. We found that organized 3D nanofiber networks facilitate pro­ton conduction by enabling intermolecular hydrogen bonding with water molecules, forming percolated ion transport channels, and leveraging the hydrophilic domains created by –SO3- groups. To enhance PEM selectivity, a novel and highly selective system was developed through S–CNF surface functionalization using layer-by-layer (LbL) and hydrophobization. These modifications effectively fill the surface and internal S–CNF matrix, forming a water-repellent barrier that reduces iodine species crossover and enhances PEM performance in ZIRFBs.
